longDescription:
Mount Parnassus, Central Greece Delphi was the most important oracle in the ancient Greek world, consulted on matters of state,
                war, colonization, and personal destiny for over a thousand years. Perched on the slopes of
                Mount Parnassus, overlooking the Gulf of Corinth, this sacred site was considered the "omphalos"
                or navel of the world. According to myth, Zeus released two eagles from opposite ends of the
                earth, and they met at Delphi, marking it as the world's center. The oracle's pronouncements,
                delivered through the Pythia priestess, shaped the course of Greek history and influenced
                decisions from Athens to Asia Minor.

name: The Oracle of Delphi
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
Perched on the slopes of
                Mount Parnassus, overlooking the Gulf of Corinth, this sacred site was considered the "omphalos"
                or navel of the world.

geography: Located at Delphi on slopes of Mount Parnassus in central Greece. Elevation ~580m. Dramatic cliff-side location. Features Castalian Spring (sacred water source). Olympic sanctuary with stadium and treasury buildings. Theatre overlooking plain
relatedEvents: Foundation of colonies on Oracle's advice
Socrates deemed wisest man in Greece

keyMyths.title: Apollo's Slaying of Python
keyMyths.description: The myth recounts how Apollo, in his youth, slew the monstrous serpent Python who guarded Delphi and was terrorizing the surrounding area. By defeating Python, Apollo established his dominion over the sanctuary and the prophetic powers associated with it.

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the Oracle
keyMyths.description: The oracle's origins are linked to Gaia (Earth), who initially possessed the prophetic powers of Delphi. Apollo later seized control, establishing the site as his own and appointing priestesses, known as Pythia, to deliver his prophecies.

keyMyths.title: Oedipus's Tragedy
keyMyths.description: Oedipus consulted the Oracle of Delphi seeking to understand his fate. The Oracle prophesied that he would kill his father and marry his mother, setting in motion the tragic events that would define his life and the history of Thebes.

keyMyths.title: Heracles and the Delphic Tripod
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the Heracles myth, he sought guidance from the Oracle but was refused. Enraged, he attempted to steal the Delphic tripod, a symbol of Apollo's authority, leading to a conflict between the hero and the god.

keyMyths.title: The Oracle and Croesus
keyMyths.description: King Croesus of Lydia tested the accuracy of various oracles by asking them to predict what he was doing on a specific day. The Oracle of Delphi was the only one to correctly answer, solidifying its reputation for truthfulness, though its prophecy regarding Croesus's war was ambiguous and led to his downfall.

sources.name: Herodotus, The Histories
sources.description: A primary source offering accounts of historical events and cultural practices, including descriptions of oracular consultations at Delphi.
sources.title: Herodotus, The Histories

sources.name: Pausanias, Description of Greece
sources.description: A guidebook to ancient Greece written in the 2nd century CE, providing detailed descriptions of Delphi's monuments, art, and religious practices.
sources.title: Pausanias, Description of Greece

sources.name: Fontenrose, Joseph Eddy. *The Delphic Oracle: Its Responses and Operations*.
sources.description: A comprehensive study analyzing the historical and religious context of the Delphic Oracle, examining its operations and the nature of its responses.
sources.title: Fontenrose, Joseph Eddy. *The Delphic Oracle: Its Responses and Operations*.

sources.name: Homeric Hymn to Apollo
sources.description: An ancient Greek hymn dedicated to Apollo, which recounts the god's birth, his slaying of Python, and his establishment of the Delphic sanctuary.
sources.title: Homeric Hymn to Apollo

sources.name: Sophocles, Oedipus Rex
sources.description: A classic Greek tragedy that features the Oracle of Delphi's prophecy as a central element in the unfolding of Oedipus's fate.
sources.title: Sophocles, Oedipus Rex
